content:
# Leviticus Chapter 26 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 74r–74v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_026_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_026_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** "Do not make idols for yourselves. Do not set up a carved image or a sacred stone, and do not place a sculpted stone in your land to bow down to it — for I am the LORD your God. **2** Keep my Sabbaths and stand in awe of my sanctuary. I am the LORD.

**3** "If you walk in my statutes and keep my commandments and carry them out, **4** I will send rain for your land at the right time — the autumn rains and the spring rains — and the land will yield its produce and the trees of the field will bear their fruit. **5** Your threshing will last until the grape harvest, and the grape harvest will last until the time for sowing. You will eat your fill of food and live securely in your land. **6** I will bring peace to the land, and you will lie down at night with nothing to frighten you. I will remove dangerous animals from the land, and the sword will not pass through your country. **7** You will pursue your enemies and cut them down before you — they will fall before your sword. **8** Five of you will chase a hundred, and a hundred of you will put ten thousand to flight. Your enemies will fall before you by the sword.

**9** "I will turn toward you — I will make you fruitful and multiply you — and I will confirm my covenant with you. **10** You will still be eating last year's harvest when you have to clear it out to make room for the new. **11** I will make my dwelling among you, and I will not turn away from you in disgust. **12** I will walk among you and be your God, and you will be my people. **13** I am the LORD your God, who brought you out from the land of Egypt, from the house of slavery, so that you would no longer be their slaves. I broke the bars of your yoke and made you walk with your heads held high.

**14** "But if you do not listen to me and do not carry out all these commandments — **15** if you reject my statutes and your soul turns away from my laws, so that you refuse to carry out all my commandments and break my covenant — **16** then this is what I will bring on you: sudden terror, wasting disease, and burning fever that consumes your eyes and drains your life away. You will sow your seed to no purpose because your enemies will eat it. **17** I will set my face against you, and you will be struck down before your enemies. Those who hate you will rule over you, and you will flee even when no one is chasing you.

**18** "If after all this you still do not listen to me, I will go on to punish you seven times over for your sins. **19** I will break down the pride you take in your own strength. I will make your sky like iron and your ground like bronze — **20** and your strength will be spent for nothing. Your land will not yield its produce, and the trees of the land will not produce their fruit.

**21** "If you continue to oppose me and refuse to listen to me, I will go on to strike you seven times harder for your sins. **22** I will send wild animals among you, and they will rob you of your children, destroy your livestock, and reduce your numbers until your roads become deserted.

**23** "If after all this discipline you still do not turn back to me, but continue to oppose me — **24** then I myself will oppose you, and I will strike you seven times harder for your sins. **25** I will bring a sword against you to execute the vengeance of the covenant. When you gather in your towns, I will send a plague among you, and you will be handed over to the enemy. **26** When I cut off your supply of bread, ten women will be able to bake your bread in a single oven and dole out your bread by weight, and you will eat — but you will not be satisfied.

**27** "If in spite of this you still do not listen to me, but continue to oppose me — **28** then in my burning anger I will oppose you and punish you seven times over for your sins. **29** You will eat the flesh of your own sons and daughters. **30** I will destroy your high places and cut down your incense altars. I will pile your corpses on the corpses of your idols, and I will turn away from you in disgust. **31** I will demolish your cities and devastate your sanctuaries, and I will not inhale the soothing fragrance of your offerings. **32** I myself will devastate the land, so that your enemies who come to live there will be appalled by it. **33** I will scatter you among the nations and draw out the sword to pursue you. Your land will be left in desolation and your cities in ruins.

**34** "Then the land will enjoy its Sabbaths — all the days of its desolation, while you are in the land of your enemies. Then the land will rest and enjoy its Sabbaths. **35** As long as it lies desolate, the land will have the rest it did not have during the Sabbaths when you lived on it.

**36** "As for those of you who are left — I will fill their hearts with such fear in the lands of their enemies that the sound of a wind-driven leaf will send them fleeing. They will flee as though from a sword, and they will fall even when no one is chasing them. **37** They will stumble over each other as though fleeing from a sword, even when no one is pursuing them. You will have no power to stand before your enemies. **38** You will perish among the nations; the land of your enemies will consume you. **39** Those of you who are left will waste away in the lands of your enemies because of their own iniquities — and because of their ancestors' iniquities, they will waste away along with them.

**40** "But if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their ancestors — their treachery against me and their opposition to me, **41** so that I was against them and brought them into the land of their enemies — if then their uncircumcised hearts are humbled and they accept the punishment for their iniquity, **42** then I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and my covenant with Isaac, and my covenant with Abraham I will also remember. And I will remember the land. **43** The land will have been abandoned by them and will have enjoyed its Sabbaths during its desolation without them. And they will accept the punishment for their iniquity, because they rejected my laws and their souls despised my statutes.

**44** "Yet in spite of all this — when they are in the land of their enemies — I will not reject them or turn away from them so as to destroy them utterly and break my covenant with them. For I am the LORD their God. **45** I will remember on their behalf the covenant with the ancestors, whom I brought out from the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, so that I might be their God. I am the LORD."

**46** These are the statutes and the judgments and the laws that the LORD established between himself and the Israelites on Mount Sinai through Moses.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### What Leviticus 26 Is Actually About

Leviticus 26 is the most architecturally intentional chapter in the Holiness Code — and one of the most powerful in the entire Torah. It is a formal covenant document, structured as a suzerain-vassal treaty: first the supreme blessing for obedience, then an escalating series of curses for disobedience, and finally a remarkable floor of grace that holds even when everything above it has collapsed. You can read it as a legal text. You can also read it as a love letter from a God who cannot bring himself to let go, even after cataloguing in devastating detail all the reasons he should.

The chapter divides cleanly:
- **Verses 1–2:** Prologue — the two foundational commands (no idols; keep Sabbaths)
- **Verses 3–13:** Blessing — obedience brings rain, harvest, security, and God's presence
- **Verses 14–39:** Curse — five escalating stages, each prefaced by "if you still do not listen"
- **Verses 40–45:** The unconditional floor — confession, divine memory, and covenant that cannot be broken
- **Verse 46:** Colophon — the legal closure of the Sinai legislation

The structural genius of the chapter is the asymmetry between blessing and curse. The blessing occupies eleven verses and culminates in the staggering gift of verse 12: "I will walk among you and be your God, and you will be my people." The curse occupies twenty-five verses and spirals into exile, cannibalism, and total desolation. But the curse section is immediately followed by seventeen verses of grace that are theologically weightier than the curses. The curse cannot be the final word. The covenant cannot be dissolved unilaterally.

### Verse 12: "I Will Walk Among You" — The Climax of the Whole

"I will walk among you and be your God, and you will be my people."

This is the apex of the entire blessing section — and arguably the apex of the Holiness Code itself. It is the goal toward which the whole system of sacrifices, purity laws, calendar observances, and ethical demands has been building. All of Leviticus is in service of one thing: making possible the dwelling of a holy God in the midst of an unholy people. And here it is, spoken plainly and personally: I will walk (*hithalakhti*) among you.

The verb *hithalekh* — the reflexive/intensive form of "to walk" — carries enormous weight in Hebrew. It is the verb used of Enoch in Genesis 5:22: "Enoch walked with God." It is used of Noah in Genesis 6:9: "Noah was a man who walked with God." In Genesis 3:8, it describes the LORD "walking in the garden in the cool of the day" — the verb that signals the terrifying intimacy of God in Eden that Adam and Eve can no longer bear after their rebellion. The same verb returns here in Leviticus 26. God promises to resume the Eden relationship: the walking-with, the dwelling-in-the-midst, the unhurried divine presence that was lost at the fall and that the entire sacrificial and holiness system is designed to make possible again.

This is why Leviticus is not a book of tedious rituals. The rituals are the scaffolding around this promise. Every blood offering, every purity regulation, every "holy to the LORD" is load-bearing infrastructure for the moment when God says: I will walk among you.

The verse echoes in both directions through Scripture. It echoes back to the Garden. It echoes forward to the Tabernacle (God's dwelling, *mishkan*, among Israel), to Solomon's Temple, to the prophetic visions of God's presence returning to a rebuilt city (Ezekiel 37:27; Zechariah 2:10). And it echoes most fully into the New Testament, where John 1:14 says of Jesus: "The Word *pitched his tent* among us" — using *eskēnōsen*, from *skēnē* (tent/tabernacle), the same word used for the Tabernacle in the LXX. The New Testament does not merely quote Leviticus 26:12; it fulfills it. God does not merely promise to walk among his people; he becomes one of them, walking on the roads of Galilee.

Revelation 21:3 closes the canon by returning to this verse explicitly: "Behold, the dwelling of God is with humanity, and he will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them and will be their God." The Greek echoes Leviticus 26:12 word for word. The new creation is the fulfillment of the Leviticus promise. All of human history from Genesis 3 to Revelation 21 is the story of God working to restore the condition Leviticus 26:12 describes: I walk among you. You are mine.

### Verses 3–13: The Shape of Blessing

The blessing section is often overlooked because the curses are so vivid. But it deserves attention as a theology of shalom — wholeness, flourishing, right order. Notice what the blessings include:

**Rain at the right time** (v.4): In an agricultural world, timely rain is the difference between life and starvation. The promise is not abundance at random but provision that fits the pattern — rain when planting needs it, rain when growth needs it. Creation ordered for human flourishing.

**Surplus that overflows** (v.5): The harvests overlap — you are still threshing grain when the grapes come in, still harvesting grapes when it is time to plant. The land produces more than you need, more than you can process. This is the image of Eden behind the promise: not bare survival but overflowing generosity.

**Security in sleep** (v.6): "You will lie down at night with nothing to frighten you." This may be the most human of all the blessings. The ability to sleep in peace — without anxiety about tomorrow, without fear of violence in the night — is a deep marker of flourishing. Its absence is among the most common characteristics of trauma. The blessing of covenant faithfulness includes the blessing of untroubled sleep.

**Multiplication, not just protection** (v.9): God will "turn toward" (*paniti aleykem*) his people — this is the same root as the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:26, "The LORD turn his face toward you." When God's face is turned toward you, you flourish; when it is turned away, you wither. Here the turning is paired with multiplication: fruitfulness, increase, presence. The covenant blessing is not merely survival but growth, not merely protection but abundance.

**Freedom from shame** (v.13): "I made you walk with your heads held high" — *qomemiyut*, a word found only here in the Hebrew Bible, meaning something like upright stature, the posture of freedom rather than the bent-neck posture of a slave under a yoke. The final blessing in the blessing section is dignity: the LORD's people walk tall.

### The Five-Stage Curse: A Theology of Escalation

The curse section is architecturally remarkable. It does not pile all the disasters together; it staggers them into five stages, each preceded by the same conditional: "If you still do not listen to me" (*ve'im lo tishme'u li*). The repetition creates a drumbeat of divine patience and human stubbornness. Each stage gives Israel a chance to turn before the next falls.

**Stage One (vv.14–17):** Disease, failed harvests, military defeat, psychological terror. Bad, but still survivable. Life continues; it is simply harsh.

**Stage Two (vv.18–20):** Drought and agricultural failure. The sky becomes iron; the ground becomes bronze. The basic relationship between the land and the people — the foundational promise of covenant — begins to break down.

**Stage Three (vv.21–22):** Wild animals reclaim the land. Children are killed. The roads empty. This stage signals the collapse of civilization itself, not just agricultural economics — when roads are deserted, trade stops, community stops, civilization stops.

**Stage Four (vv.23–26):** Plague and military siege. Bread rationed by weight. The image of ten women baking in one oven (v.26) is an image of extreme scarcity — a reduction in population so severe, or a reduction in fuel and grain so complete, that the whole community shares what should serve one household. Eating without being satisfied (v.26) is covenant curse language that mirrors the covenant blessing of eating your fill (v.5). The same verbs, the same actions, the opposite result.

**Stage Five (vv.27–39):** Total catastrophe. Cannibalism. The destruction of the high places that were supposed to be places of worship. Exile. The land left desolate. And the psychological horror of verse 36: "the sound of a wind-driven leaf will send them fleeing." People so traumatized by the totality of disaster that even the ordinary sounds of nature have become terror. The wind in the leaves, which in the garden was the sound accompanying God's walking-in-the-evening, now sends Israel running in panic from an enemy that isn't there.

This is not sadism. It is covenant logic. If the blessing is the presence of God, then the curse is the progressive removal of everything that makes human life possible — because human life without God is, as the chapter shows, a trajectory toward dissolution. The curses are not arbitrary punishments; they are the natural consequence of choosing to live as though God is not there.

### Verse 34–35: The Land's Sabbaths

A remarkable moment in the curse section: when Israel is exiled, the land gets its Sabbaths. "Then the land will enjoy its Sabbaths — all the days of its desolation, while you are in the land of your enemies."

This is the explanation given in 2 Chronicles 36:21 for the Babylonian exile: "to fulfill the word of the LORD by the mouth of Jeremiah, until the land had enjoyed its Sabbaths. All the days that it lay desolate it kept Sabbath, to fulfill seventy years."

The theology here is quietly devastating. Israel had refused to let the land rest as commanded. The land did not get its Sabbath years. And so God enforces the Sabbath by removing the people entirely. The land rests because it is emptied. This is the irony at the heart of the exile: Israel lost the land precisely because they treated the land as something that existed for their use, rather than as something that exists in relationship with God and must be allowed to breathe. When you refuse to let the land keep Sabbath, the land will get its Sabbath anyway — over your absence.

There is a warning here for any people who treat the land as infinitely exploitable. Creation has its own rights, its own relationship with its Creator. A theology that includes the land's Sabbath is a theology that takes creation's flourishing seriously, not merely human flourishing.

### Verse 42: The Reverse Patriarchal Memory

"Then I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and my covenant with Isaac, and my covenant with Abraham I will also remember."

The normal order in Torah for invoking the patriarchal covenant is Abraham, Isaac, Jacob — the historical order. Here it is reversed: Jacob, Isaac, Abraham. This is not a scribal error. It is a deliberate rhetorical move. God walks backward through history the way a person walks backward when they are trying to remember the very beginning — going further and further back until they reach the foundation.

By starting with Jacob (the most recent, the most complicated, the one who wrestled with God at the Jabbok, the one whose children became the twelve tribes now in exile), God is saying: I remember even Jacob — the trickster, the one who had to be broken before he could be blessed. Then further back: I remember Isaac. Further still: I remember Abraham, the first, the one to whom I made the promise at the very beginning.

The backward walk through the patriarchs is a walk back to the unconditional promise. The Abrahamic covenant in Genesis 15 is one-sided: God alone walked through the cut animals (Gen 15:17). It was not "if you obey me, I will bless you" — it was "I will bless you." The progressive movement from Jacob to Isaac to Abraham is a movement from the most recent conditional history back to the most ancient unconditional promise. Even exile cannot reach that deep. Even the full unfolding of all five curse stages cannot reach back to undo what God did in Genesis 15.

### Verse 44: The Unconditional Floor

"Yet in spite of all this — when they are in the land of their enemies — I will not reject them or turn away from them so as to destroy them utterly and break my covenant with them. For I am the LORD their God."

This is the hinge verse of the entire chapter — and one of the most important verses in the Torah. After twenty-five verses of escalating devastation, after the full unfolding of all five curse stages, after exile, desolation, cannibalism, and the terror of wind-driven leaves, God draws a line that cannot be crossed: I will not reject them utterly. I will not break my covenant.

The Hebrew is emphatic: *lo-ma'astim ve-lo-ge'altim le-khalotam* — "I did not reject them and I did not abhor them to destroy them completely." The double negative, followed by the infinitive "completely," signals a negation that holds against every pressure. Not in these conditions. Not ever. Not even here.

The word *me'as* — reject, despise — is the same word used in 1 Samuel 15:23, 26 when God rejects Saul from being king. God does that to Saul. He does not do that to Israel. And *ga'al* — abhor, turn away from in disgust — is used elsewhere of the most intense divine revulsion. God says: not even that. Not even the strongest form of rejection I know how to feel.

The theological weight of this verse is enormous for the entire history of Israel's relationship with God in the Hebrew Bible. The exilic prophets — Isaiah 40–55 especially — draw on exactly this promise when they announce comfort to a people in Babylon who believe they have been abandoned: "For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with deep compassion I will gather you again" (Isa 54:7). The Lamentations poet in the ashes of Jerusalem holds on to precisely the promise of Leviticus 26:44: "For no one is cast off by the Lord forever" (Lam 3:31). The prophets preach to the exile, and the exile's people hold on, because Leviticus 26:44 is in the covenant document. God himself, in advance, put the floor in.

### Verses 40–45: Confession and Covenant Memory

The path back from exile in verses 40–45 passes through confession: "If they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their ancestors." This is not a new earning of favor; it is a clearing of the obstacle. The covenant was never revoked from God's side. The issue is that Israel has been living as though it was. Confession is the act of acknowledging the reality that has been there all along: we are the covenant people of a covenant God who has not abandoned us, and we have acted as though none of this were true.

"If their uncircumcised hearts are humbled" (v.41) — this phrase is striking. Circumcision was the physical sign of covenant membership; an uncircumcised heart is a heart that has never really taken the covenant inside, that bears only the external mark without the internal reality. The prophets later make this the center of their critique and their hope: Jeremiah 4:4 calls for the circumcision of the heart; Deuteronomy 30:6 promises that God himself will circumcise their hearts. The path from exile to restoration runs through the inside.

"I will remember the land" (v.42) — God's remembering is a covenant action. In the Bible, God "remembering" is not a cognitive act (as if he had forgotten); it is a covenantal act of renewed attention and intervention. When God "remembered" Noah (Gen 8:1), the flood waters receded. When God "remembered" Rachel (Gen 30:22), she conceived. When God "remembered" his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Exod 2:24), the Exodus began. To be remembered by God is to be moved toward. When God says here "I will remember the land," he is promising the return: the people will come home, the land will be restored, creation will be healed.

### Verse 46: The Colophon — "On Mount Sinai Through Moses"

"These are the statutes and the judgments and the laws that the LORD established between himself and the Israelites on Mount Sinai through Moses."

This closing verse functions as the formal subscription of the entire Holiness Code — the legal closer that seals the legislation from Leviticus 17 through 26 as a unified document. The Hebrew uses three distinct terms: *chuqqim* (statutes — the authoritative rules), *mishpatim* (judgments — the case-law precedents), and *torot* (laws/teachings — the general instruction). All three categories, together, make up the comprehensive covenant document.

"Between himself and the Israelites" — the phrase is bilateral and relational. This is not merely a divine decree; it is a covenant between parties, an agreement that has two sides. God gives his obligations: rain, fertility, presence, ultimate faithfulness. Israel gives its obligations: obedience, exclusive loyalty, Sabbath-keeping, social ethics. The Holiness Code is the covenant document. Mount Sinai is the location of its ratification. Moses is its mediator.

The mention of Mount Sinai is unusual in Leviticus (cf. 25:1) — most of the Leviticus legislation is given at the Tent of Meeting. The singling out of Sinai for the colophon of the Holiness Code signals the legal seriousness and finality of this collection. These laws have the full authority of the Sinai revelation. And the mention of Moses as mediator places the Holiness Code in its canonical location: the covenant made at the mountain, mediated through the greatest of all prophets, binding on all who stand within the community of Israel — which is to say, binding in one way or another on all who read these words as scripture.

### The LXX in Vaticanus (Pages 112–113)

The Greek of Leviticus 26 in Vaticanus follows the Hebrew closely while making a number of characteristic LXX choices. Several are notable:

The Greek for verse 12 — *kai emperipatēsō en hymin* — "I will walk about among you" — uses *emperipatēō*, the compound form of walking, which emphasizes the walking-around, walking-through, walking-in-the-midst quality of the divine presence. The LXX verb is also the root behind Paul's use of "walking" as a metaphor for the Christian life (*peripatein*, to walk, to conduct oneself): "Walk in a manner worthy of your calling" (Eph 4:1). Paul's walking language is not an accident; it is saturated with the Leviticus promise of God walking among his people, and with the implication that to walk with God is to walk as God walks.

The Greek for "I will not reject them utterly" in verse 44 uses *ou mē bdelychthō* — the double negative aorist subjunctive, expressing the strongest possible denial that an action will take place. "I will absolutely not abhor them." The LXX here is if anything more emphatic than the Hebrew in piling up negations. The Greek Bible also wants its readers to be in no doubt: the floor holds.

The reverse patriarchal order in verse 42 — Jacob, Isaac, Abraham — is preserved exactly in the LXX. Whatever the rhetorical strategy behind the inversion, both the Hebrew and Greek traditions transmit it faithfully, signaling that it is not an accident to be corrected but a deliberate theological move to be heard.

The word *aphesis* (release) does not appear in chapter 26 — that is the vocabulary of chapter 25's Jubilee. Chapter 26 instead uses the vocabulary of covenant (*diathēkē*), memory (*mimnēskomai*), and rejection (*bdelyssomai*, *apōtheomai*). The LXX of verse 45 is particularly important: "I will remember for them the covenant of those who came before (*archaiōn*), whom I brought out from the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, to be their God." The "covenant of the ancients" — the Abrahamic promise — is the foundation that survives exile. It is *archē*, original, prior, foundational. It cannot be undone by what happens in the middle of the story.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 74r–74v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 112–113 (LXX).*
